content.problemStatement:
There are NNN people standing in a line. The iii-th person from the left has a strength of AiA_iAi​.

It is guaranteed that Ai=1A_i = 1Ai​=1 or Ai=2A_i = 2Ai​=2 for each person.

Suppose person iii is holding a ball. They can throw the ball to person jjj **if and only if** AiA_iAi​ divides ∣i−j∣|i - j|∣i−j∣, i.e. if the strength of person iii divides the distance between persons iii and jjj.

Your task is to find whether there exists a pair of people iii and jjj such that if person iii is initially holding a ball, there is no sequence of zero or more throws that will allow for person jjj to receive the ball.


Input Format

The first line of input will contain a single integer TTT, denoting the number of test cases.
Each test case consists of two lines of input.

The first line of each test case contains a single integer NNN — the number of people in the line.
The second line contains NNN space-separated integers A1,A2,…,ANA_1, A_2, \ldots, A_NA1​,A2​,…,AN​ — the strengths of the people.




Output Format
For each test case, on a new line, output the answer: `Yes` if there exists a valid pair (i,j)(i, j)(i,j) such that the ball starting with iii means it can never reach jjj, and `No` otherwise.


Each character of the output may be printed in either uppercase or lowercase, i.e. the strings `NO`, `No`, `nO`, and `no` will all be treated as equivalent.
constraints: The sum of NNN over all test cases won't exceed 2⋅1052\cdot 10^52⋅105.

sampleTestCases.explanation:
Explanation:
Test case 111: Person 222 has strength 222, and so cannot actually throw the ball to either person 111 or person 333.
Test case 222: Both people can throw the ball to each other, so no valid pair exists.
Test case 333: Person 222 can only throw the ball to person 444, while person 444 can only throw to person 222.
So, if one of these two holds the ball, they can only throw it back-and-forth, but they'll never be able to make the ball reach persons 111 or 333.
